name: Muse Spark 1.1 (xhigh)
archDisplay: Proprietary multimodal reasoning model (undisclosed architecture), Meta Superintelligence Labs' first model family, update to April 2026 Muse Spark
note: First flagship from Meta Superintelligence Labs (led by Alexandr Wang), replacing the open Llama line with a proprietary model; base Muse Spark launched 2026-04-08, this is the 1.1 update from 2026-07-09, evaluated here at the 'xhigh' reasoning-effort tier. Meta has said it hopes to open-source future versions but this release is closed/API-only.

results.notes: Humanity's Last Exam, Artificial Analysis independent evaluation of Muse Spark 1.1
Agentic computer-use benchmark, 361-task split, xhigh reasoning effort; Figure 44 of Meta's official evaluation report
Sourced from Scale AI leaderboard per Meta's own report, mini-swe-agent harness
Terminal-Bench 2.1, pass@1 averaged over 5 attempts on 89 tasks, bash-tool-only agent harness
GDPval-AA v2 Elo, sourced from the Artificial Analysis GDPval-AA Leaderboard per Meta's own report
CyberGym pass@1, real-world cybersecurity vulnerability discovery/exploitation, from Table 1 of Meta's official Preparedness Assessment (helpful-only variant)

CharXiv Reasoning subset (1,000 chart-reasoning questions from the CharXiv validation set, matching this corpus's convention of using the reasoning subset for the 'charxiv' slug), run with a code-execution tool, graded by gpt-oss-120b as LLM-judge for semantic/mathematical equivalence; Figure 44 of Meta's official Muse Spark 1.1 Evaluation Report, Section 5.2.4.

GPQA Diamond, measured independently by Artificial Analysis as one of the 9 evaluations in their Intelligence Index v4.1 breakdown. Cross-checked by reading the same figure from two separate AA comparison-basket renders (Muse Spark 1.1's own page and Mistral Large 3's page, which both include Muse Spark 1.1 in their pinned comparison set) -- both show 90%.
LiveCodeBench, measured independently by Vals AI (±0.99 CI, ranked 18/127 models on their leaderboard). Not reported anywhere in Meta's own materials for Muse Spark 1.1.

MMLU-Pro, measured independently by Vals AI (±0.32 CI, ranked 11/120 models on their leaderboard). Meta has not published an MMLU or MMLU-Pro score for Muse Spark 1.1 in either the launch blog or the Evaluation Report.
MMMU, measured independently by Vals AI (±0.82 CI, ranked 11/82 models on their leaderboard).

SWE-bench (base/Verified-style variant, per this corpus's existing convention for the 'swe-bench' slug), measured independently by Vals AI (±1.72 CI, ranked 8/69 models). Distinct from the 'swe-bench-pro' slug already recorded for this model, which is Scale AI's separate, harder SWE-Bench Pro benchmark self-reported by Meta at 61.5.

Humanity's Last Exam, no-tools configuration, full 2,500-question set, GPT o3-mini as LLM-judge, xhigh reasoning effort; Figure 44 ('General capability benchmark results') of Meta's official Muse Spark 1.1 Evaluation Report, Section 5.2.5. Distinct from the with-tools score of 62.1 (also recorded separately) and from this model's existing independent Artificial Analysis HLE measurement of 45% already in this model's results.

Terminal-Bench v2.1, measured independently by Artificial Analysis as one of the 9 evaluations in their Intelligence Index v4.1 breakdown. Notably lower than Meta's own self-reported 80.0 (already recorded for this model) and Vals AI's independently-measured 69.29 (also recorded here), illustrating the self-report/independent-eval gap on this benchmark.

GDPval-AA v2 Elo, Artificial Analysis's own independently-run measurement (article states '+232 Elo (1144 to 1376)' vs. the original April-2026 Muse Spark). Distinct from the 1381 already recorded for this model, which is Meta's self-reported figure relayed from a separate Scale AI leaderboard number -- two different measurements of the same benchmark from two different evaluators.

SealedBench sealed capability index — composite of capability, token efficiency, speed and cost; rank 12/28 on the 2026-07-22 run (sealedbench.com). Listed as "Muse Spark 1.1" (Meta). Evaluated at the highest available thinking setting. Attached to the xhigh effort sibling of Muse Spark 1.1 (highest available thinking).

verificationNotes:
Base Muse Spark's own April 2026 release announcement (via Meta AI blog, relayed by buildfastwithai.com) reports Intelligence Index v4.0 52, HealthBench Hard 42.8, HLE 50.2%, Terminal-Bench 2.0 59.0, ARC-AGI-2 42.5 -- these are self-reported. Artificial Analysis independently measured Muse Spark 1.1 (xhigh) at Intelligence Index 51, a +8pt gain over the original that AA attributes mostly to Coding (+12) and HLE (+5, to 45%).

2026-07-16: Checked for missing mmlu/math/aime/humaneval scores. Fetched and full-text-extracted (pdftotext) Meta's 112-page official Muse Spark 1.1 Evaluation Report (ai.meta.com/static-resource/muse-spark-1-1-evaluation-report) -- zero occurrences of "MMLU", "MATH", "AIME", or "HumanEval" anywhere in the document; its Section 5.2 "General Capabilities" breakdown covers only Agents, Coding (Terminal-Bench 2.1, SWE-Bench Pro, DeepSWE v1.1, VibeCodeBench v1.1), Health, Multimodal, and Reasoning (HLE, MRCR v2) -- no legacy knowledge/math/code benchmarks at all. Also checked both official launch blogs (ai.meta.com/blog/introducing-muse-spark-meta-model-api for the 1.1 update, and the original ai.meta.com/blog/introducing-muse-spark-msl for base Muse Spark) -- neither mentions any of the four benchmarks. Cross-checked independent leaderboards: Vals AI (vals.ai/models/meta_muse-spark-1.1) lists 23 benchmark tiles, none of which is MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval; Artificial Analysis's current Intelligence Index v4.1 for this model explicitly enumerates its 9 component evals (GDPval-AA v2, tau3-Banking, Terminal-Bench v2.1, SciCode, HLE, GPQA Diamond, CritPt, AA-Omniscience, AA-LCR) -- none of the four legacy benchmarks are among them, consistent with AA having moved off saturated evals. No sibling Muse Spark model exists elsewhere in this corpus to cross-reference. All four are treated as genuine non-publication, not a research gap -- consistent with the broader pattern of frontier labs no longer reporting saturated legacy benchmarks like MMLU/MATH/HumanEval.

2026-07-16: Re-checked the swe-bench=82 (Vals AI) comparability flag. This is a false positive from audit-data.ts's documented negation-blind text-matching (see dataset-audit-2026-07 notes): the row's own notes already explicitly state the score is "Distinct from the 'swe-bench-pro' slug," not a claim that it IS SWE-Bench Pro. A fresh fetch of vals.ai/models/meta_muse-spark-1.1 confirms only one "SWE-bench" tile exists on that page (rank 9/70) with no separate "SWE-Bench Pro" tile, and several other corpus rows sourced from Vals AI (e.g. Qwen3.5-Plus, Grok 4.5) independently confirm and document that Vals AI's "SWE-bench" tile is specifically SWE-bench Verified, matching this corpus's swe-bench slug definition ("SWE-bench Verified" per corpus/benchmarks.json). No change made -- the row was already correctly labeled.

2026-07-17: Second-pass deep research for missing mmlu/math/aime/humaneval, deliberately avoiding the source types the first pass already exhausted (official launch materials, Epoch AI, Vals AI, LMArena, BenchLM) and instead checking six new source categories:\n\n1. Stanford HELM. crfm.stanford.edu's Classic/Lite/Capabilities leaderboards render client-side so were checked via targeted web search rather than raw fetch; no \"Muse Spark\" entry found anywhere on HELM. Critically, a search result confirms \"HELM entered maintenance mode on June 1, 2026\" -- five weeks before Muse Spark 1.1 even released (2026-07-09) -- so HELM structurally cannot have evaluated this model. Ruled out with high confidence.\n\n2. arXiv / academic papers. Fetched and full-text-searched (via pdftotext on the downloaded PDF) the \"Muse Spark Safety & Preparedness Report\" (arxiv.org/abs/2606.12429, 2.7MB, 7716 lines of extracted text): MMLU appears only as a \"Control\" evaluation baseline for measuring selective-underperformance/sandbagging deltas (Tables 15-16) -- no absolute MMLU score is ever stated, and MATH/AIME/HumanEval do not appear anywhere in the document (verified with separate greps for each term). Also checked two papers that use Muse Spark as a baseline comparison point (MCP-Atlas, arxiv.org/html/2602.00933, reports only an MCP-Atlas pass rate of 82.2% for Muse Spark, no cross-benchmark scores; GIM, arxiv.org/html/2605.18663v1, mentions Muse Spark only for GIM's own spatial/intuitive-reasoning categories). Also checked \"Every Eval Ever\" (arxiv.org/pdf/2606.14516), a paper specifically about a community eval-results repository -- it does not mention Muse Spark at all, and its linked repos (github.com/evaleval/every_eval_ever, huggingface.co/datasets/evaleval/EEE_datastore) were not independently found to contain a Muse Spark entry via search.\n\n3. GitHub eval-harness logs. Found one GitHub repo referencing Muse Spark (kamellperry/meta-muse-spark-api) -- confirmed via fetch that it is only an OpenAI-compatible API wrapper/proxy with no committed benchmark results, eval logs, or results.json. Broader searches for lm-evaluation-harness results against \"muse-spark\" returned nothing.\n\n4. Other leaderboards/aggregators. (a) OpenRouter's model page for meta/muse-spark-1.1 (read live via browser, not just fetch) shows a \"Benchmarks\" section explicitly labeled \"Metrics sourced from Artificial Analysis\" containing Intelligence/Coding/Agentic indices plus GPQA Diamond, HLE, AA-LCR, GDPval-AA, CritPt, SciCode, AA-Omniscience -- i.e. the same 9-eval AA Intelligence Index v4.1 breakdown the first pass already checked, confirming no MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval there either. (b) LiveBench.ai (read live via browser, LiveBench-2026-06-25 release): \"Muse Spark 1.1 xHigh Effort\" IS listed (Overall 76.2) with a Mathematics-category subtask breakdown of AMPS Hard 82.0, \"integrals with game\" 83.0, math comp 96.1, olympiad 87.5, and a Coding-category breakdown of code generation 76.1 / code completion 78.3 -- but these are LiveBench's own proprietary, contamination-free reformulated tasks (explicitly designed to avoid reusing the original MATH/AIME/HumanEval question sets), not the canonical MATH-500/AIME/HumanEval benchmarks this corpus's benchmarks.json defines by those slugs, so they are not comparable/substitutable data points. (c) Scale AI's SEAL leaderboard (labs.scale.com/leaderboard, read live via browser): enumerated all ~26 current benchmark categories (DrugDiscoveryBench, SWE Atlas x3, HiL-Bench, MCP Atlas, SWE-Bench Pro Public/Private, SciPredict, HLE x2, AudioMultiChallenge x3, Professional Reasoning Finance/Legal, Remote Labor Index, PropensityBench, VisualToolBench, MultiNRC, MultiChallenge, Fortress, MASK, EnigmaEval, VISTA, TutorBench) -- none is MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval, and Muse Spark 1.1 appears on several of these (MCP Atlas 88.10, SWE-Bench Pro Public/Private, HLE, Professional Reasoning, VisualToolBench, MultiNRC, MultiChallenge, Fortress) but never on a legacy-benchmark row because none exists on this leaderboard. (d) AGI Ranker (agiranker.com/model/muse-spark) aggregates AA/BenchLM/Vals/LiveBench data for base Muse Spark -- 9 benchmark tiles, none of the four targets. (e) pricepertoken.com's dedicated MMLU leaderboard (\"Benchmarks from Artificial Analysis\", 79 models ranked) was also checked live -- it lists old Llama models (Llama 4 Maverick/Scout, Llama 3.1/3.2) under Meta but no Muse Spark or Muse Spark 1.1 entry at all, corroborating that AA has no MMLU figure for this model to report anywhere.\n\n5. Non-English sources. Chinese-language search (Zhihu, Weibo, DataLearnerAI, Baidu Baike) surfaced multiple Muse Spark launch-discussion articles; direct fetch of zhuanlan.zhihu.com and datalearner.com was blocked (403 / DNS-blocked in this environment), but cross-referencing multiple independent search-result summaries of these Chinese sources shows they only discuss the same self-reported figures already in this corpus (HLE, CharXiv Reasoning 86.4, HealthBench Hard 42.8) -- no MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval number surfaces in any Chinese-language source found. (Note: several English SEO/content-mill blog posts, e.g. mindstudio.ai, tokencalculator.com, aitooltier.com, repeatedly assert vague unsourced claims like \"Muse Spark 1.1 scores comparable to GPT-5.5 on MMLU, HumanEval, MATH, and GPQA\" or a specific \"97.0% on AIME 2025\" -- these were deliberately NOT used as they cite no methodology, no primary source, and are not one of the six approved new source categories; they read as templated/generated comparison content rather than genuine measurements.)\n\n6. Sibling models. Confirmed via `find` over corpus/models/ that no other Muse Spark variant (base, non-xhigh effort tiers, etc.) exists anywhere else in this corpus to cross-reference for a comparison-table score.\n\nConclusion: all four benchmarks (mmlu, math, aime, humaneval) remain genuinely unpublished for Muse Spark 1.1 (xhigh) across every new source type checked. No number added for any of the four; this is treated as confirmed non-publication, not a research gap, consistent with the first pass's same conclusion and the broader pattern of frontier labs (and, apparently, most independent evaluators tracking this specific model) having moved off these saturated legacy benchmarks for 2026-era frontier releases.

2026-07-18: Third-pass deep research for missing mmlu/math/aime/humaneval, using only the five NEW source types specified for this round (Wayback Machine, conference talks/keynote/podcast transcripts, investor/press materials, GitHub issues/SDK-repo discussions, and not-yet-checked third-party review/analysis sites), deliberately avoiding every source category already exhausted in rounds 1-2.

1. WAYBACK MACHINE. Genuinely attempted 8 separate times over ~10 minutes of real elapsed time, using multiple methods: the wayback/available API (succeeded once, at http://archive.org/wayback/available?url=ai.meta.com/blog/introducing-muse-spark-meta-model-api&timestamp=20260709, confirming a snapshot exists at timestamp 20260710003820), the CDX search API (for both the official launch blog and artificialanalysis.ai/models/muse-spark-1-1, targeting snapshots from just after the 2026-07-09 release), and direct snapshot-content fetches (both the standard replay URL and the raw `id_` suffix form). Every attempt to retrieve actual page *content* from web.archive.org -- as opposed to the lightweight availability-check endpoint -- returned HTTP 429 "Too Many Requests" from archive.org's own servers, persistently, across the entire session; the WebFetch tool additionally refuses web.archive.org URLs outright ("Claude Code is unable to fetch from web.archive.org"). An alternate memento aggregator (timetravel.mementoweb.org) was also tried as a fallback and was unreachable (connection timeout, likely defunct). This source type could therefore not be completed -- it is BLOCKED by infrastructure-level rate-limiting in this environment, not a negative finding. Flagging this honestly rather than either skipping the source type or claiming a clean check.

2. CONFERENCE TALKS / KEYNOTE / PODCAST TRANSCRIPTS. Checked Simon Willison's launch-day blog post (simonwillison.net/2026/Jul/9/muse-spark-1-1/) -- his hands-on evaluation was a plugin build and a "pelican riding a bicycle" SVG test, no standardized benchmark numbers. Checked the Core Memory podcast episode 71 recap (corememory.com/p/metas-ai-chief-alex-wang-muse-spark-ai-wars), Alexandr Wang's first public interview post-launch (also covering the April base Muse Spark) -- no MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval figures mentioned anywhere in the recap. Fetched the full Hacker News launch-discussion thread (news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48846184) via the HN Algolia API (108KB of JSON, all comments) and grepped it case-insensitively for "mmlu", "humaneval", "aime", and "math" -- zero matches across the entire thread. No Meta Connect/developer-conference keynote video was found; the model was announced via an X/Twitter thread (Alexandr Wang, @alexandr_wang) plus the written blog + PDF evaluation report, not a stage presentation. Ruled out.

3. INVESTOR / PRESS MATERIALS. Checked multiple press/financial outlets covering Meta's stock reaction to the Muse Spark 1.1 launch (Globe and Mail, Yahoo Finance, CNBC, TechTimes "Meta Builds AI Revenue Stack" and "Meta Launches Paid AI API" articles) -- coverage centers on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index (51), API pricing ($1.25/$4.25 per M tokens), the 15% weekly stock surge, and unrelated infrastructure news (Iris custom silicon, Alberta data center); none cites MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval. No Meta earnings-call transcript was located that cites any of the four benchmarks by name for Muse Spark 1.1 specifically (coverage frames the API launch as a monetization story, not a benchmark-disclosure moment). Ruled out.

4. GITHUB ISSUES / SDK REPO DISCUSSIONS. Searched GitHub's issue search API (`gh api search/issues -f q='"Muse Spark" MMLU'`) -- the only hits were unrelated AI-news-aggregator repos (agents-radar, ki-news-aggregator) and an Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index v4.0 methodology review (hammer/labs#27, which is about the Index generally and does not mention Muse Spark or the four target benchmarks). Searched GitHub Discussions via the GraphQL search API for the same query -- zero results. Confirmed the community-built kamellperry/meta-muse-spark-api repo (already found in round 2) remains just an OpenAI-compatible API proxy with no benchmark data, and Meta has no official public SDK repo with issues/discussions enabled specifically for Muse Spark (meta-llama/llama-api-python exists but no matching issues found). Ruled out.

5. THIRD-PARTY REVIEW/ANALYSIS SITES not yet checked. SemiAnalysis's "The Future of Meta Superintelligence: A 1 Year Progress Update" (newsletter.semianalysis.com/p/the-future-of-meta-superintelligence) states only a qualitative comparison -- "We briefly tested Muse Spark 1.1 before the official release and believe it's roughly on par with Opus 4.6 or GLM 5.2 for general agentic use cases" -- no numeric benchmark of any kind, let alone the four targets. Also checked several newly-surfaced blogs/content sites not part of rounds 1-2: kingy.ai (full 18-row benchmark table, no MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval), datacamp.com's two Muse Spark articles (same self-reported table, notes "Meta Internal Coding Bench" score withheld as non-reproducible, no legacy-benchmark numbers), mindstudio.ai (explicitly defines what MMLU/HumanEval/MATH/GPQA are in a generic explainer paragraph, then asserts Muse Spark 1.1 "scores on par with GPT-5.5" on them with zero citation, methodology, or actual number -- read in full and confirmed to contain no extractable score, consistent with round 2's assessment that this class of SEO content is templated filler, not a data source), and a Medium "I tested Muse Spark for a week" first-person review (medium.com/@pixipace) -- confirmed the author ran no independent MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval evaluation themselves and only cited Artificial Analysis's Intelligence Index/GDPval-AA figures (already in this corpus). kie.ai and lushbinary.com blog posts returned HTTP 403 and could not be read. Ruled out (nothing found in the readable sources).

Conclusion: 4 of the 5 new source types (keynote/podcast, investor/press, GitHub, third-party review sites) were genuinely checked and found no MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval score for Muse Spark 1.1 anywhere -- fully consistent with rounds 1 and 2. The 5th (Wayback Machine) could not be completed due to persistent archive.org rate-limiting (HTTP 429) throughout this session, despite 8 attempts via multiple methods over roughly 10 minutes; one snapshot's existence was confirmed via the lightweight availability API but its content was never retrievable. No number added for any of the four benchmarks. Combined with two prior clean passes across a total of now 15+ distinct source categories (official materials, Epoch AI, Vals AI, LMArena, BenchLM, llm-stats.com, Stanford HELM, arXiv/academic papers, GitHub eval-harness logs, MathArena, LiveBench, OpenRouter, Scale SEAL, regional-language sources, sibling-model tables, plus this round's keynote/podcast, investor/press, GitHub issues, and third-party sites), this is treated as a durable, genuine non-publication for all four benchmarks -- not a research gap. No further rounds are likely to be productive short of Meta publishing new materials or the Wayback Machine becoming accessible in a future session.
